narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model predicted that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your application. Let's explore the factors that influenced this prediction.

Your academic performance, as indicated by your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, placed you in the lowest decile compared to your peers, who typically fall around the middle decile. This suggests that your grades were significantly lower than average, which was a major factor in the model's prediction. Additionally, your LSAT score was slightly below the average, which also contributed to the prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam.

Your family income, categorized as upper-middle, was higher than the average, which generally would have been a positive factor. However, in this case, it was not enough to outweigh the influence of your academic performance. Your undergraduate GPA was slightly below average, which further pushed the prediction towards a potential failure.

Other factors, such as your racial background and full-time status, had less influence on the prediction. Being male, which is the more common gender among applicants, also had minimal impact on the outcome.

Given these insights, focusing on improving your academic performance could be beneficial. Consider seeking additional support or resources to enhance your understanding and skills in areas where you may be struggling. Improving your LSAT score and GPA could significantly increase your chances of passing the bar exam in the future. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive outcome but rather an opportunity to identify areas for growth and improvement. With dedication and effort, you can work towards achieving your goal of passing the bar exam and gaining admission to your desired university.

Wishing you the best in your future endeavors,

[Your Name]
